aiTitle: Build Claude session review visualization tool

content.text:
This overlaps heavily with your existing **session-decision-tree** project (design locked, unbuilt, at `~/Desktop/projects/session-decision-tree`) — that one parses a linear Claude session into a decision tree of forks and dropped ideas, which is almost exactly your "trace the different decisions" north star. My first question is whether this is that project evolving or a fresh start.

On the research side, good news: both halves have real literature. For **analysis**, the relevant bodies of work are design rationale capture (IBIS/gIBIS — Conklin; QOC — MacLean et al.), the Pirolli & Card sens…[truncated]

questions.question: Is this the same project as your existing session-decision-tree (design locked at ~/Desktop/projects/session-decision-tree), or a fresh start?

options.label: Evolve session-decision-tree
options.description: Treat this as v2 of that design — revisit its locked decisions against research, keep what holds up.

options.label: Fresh project
options.description: Start clean; the old design may inform it but carries no weight.

options.label: Not sure — compare first
options.description: I re-read the old design doc and report where it agrees/conflicts with this vision before you decide.

questions.question: What powers the analysis step? This determines cost, privacy, and what 'research-backed' can mean in practice.

options.label: LLM-backed (Recommended)
options.description: Claude reads the transcript and extracts decisions/rationale into a research-grounded schema (e.g., QOC: Questions, Options, Criteria). Highest quality; needs a key or runs via a Claude Code skill on your subscription.

options.label: Deterministic parsing only
options.description: Pure structural parsing of the JSONL (turns, tool calls, file edits, interruptions). Free, private, works offline in the SPA — but can't infer rationale, only surface structure.

options.label: Hybrid
options.description: Deterministic skeleton always; LLM enrichment layered on when available. More work, best of both.

questions.question: Who is the reviewer you're designing for? This changes the display priorities a lot.

options.label: Me, right after a session
options.description: Refresh-my-own-memory use case: emphasis on speed, dropped ideas, and 'what did I actually approve'.

options.label: Someone who wasn't there
options.description: Teammate/reviewer catching up cold: emphasis on narrative, context, and linking decisions to code changes.

options.label: Both
options.description: Design for the cold reader; the after-session case is a subset.

questions.question: What's the first deliverable you want from me?

options.label: Research report first (Recommended)
options.description: I survey the analysis + visualization literature and propose a cited method for each, before any code. Matches your 'not vibes' requirement.

options.label: Design doc directly
options.description: I fold research into a full design doc (architecture + analysis schema + UI spec) in one pass.

options.label: Prototype now
options.description: Skip formal research; build a quick SPA on real session JSONL and iterate.

results:
I found several relevant results related to visualizing LLM agents and sensemaking tools presented at CHI and UIST conferences. Here are some key findings:

## Inverted Pyramid Writing Structure

In journalism, the inverted pyramid refers to a story structure where the most important information (or what might even be considered the conclusion) is presented first. The who, what, when, where and why appear at the start of a story, followed by supporting details and background information.

## Progressive Disclosure Overview

Progressive disclosure and staged disclosure are both strategies to manage the profusion of features and options in modern user interfaces. Progressive disclosure defers advanced or rarely used features to a secondary screen, making applications easier to learn and less error-prone.

Calibrate treatment, not whether to design. A doc deserves the same craft as a landing page — what changes is the treatment that craft is delivered in.

## Study Overview

This paper experimentally investigates the role of visual complexity (VC) and prototypicality (PT) as design factors of websites, shaping users' first impressions by means of two studies.

In the first study, 119 screenshots of real websites varying in VC (low vs. medium vs. high) and PT (low vs. high) were rated on perceived aesthetics. Screenshot presentation time was varied as a between-subject factor (50 ms vs. 500 ms vs. 1000 ms).

## Defining Features of Minimalism

Nielsen Norman Group's analysis of 112 minimalist websites revealed the defining features of minimalism: flat design, limited color schemes, few UI elements, use of negative space, and dramatic typography.

## Flat Design

According to a Nielsen Norman Group assessment, 96 percent of minimalist websites were flat. Minimalist UI design avoids highlights, gradients, and shadows that make elements look three-dimensional.

New project (2026-07-13): app for fast human review of a Claude Code session — extract design decisions and let a reviewer trace them. North star: fast human understanding. Deliberately kept **separate** from [[project-session-decision-tree]] (which may later plug in as a "lens").

Decisions made 2026-07-13:
…[truncated]

lines: - Hybrid analysis: deterministic parsing for MVP1 (episodes segmented by user turns, explicit decisions from AskUserQuestion/plan approvals, friction from interruptions/corrections), LLM enrichment in Phase 2 (QOC rationale inference, AgentLens-style interval summaries + embedding change-point segmentation, Wikum recursive summary tree).
- Core schema constant across phases: Decision = {question, options, criteria, resolution, decider, evidence_turns} (QOC — MacLean et al. 1991).
- Display: overview+detail (Cockburn 2009) with martini-glass narrative flow (Segel & Heer 2010); linear chronological timeline (Brehmer 2017); audience is both self-recall and cold readers.

+- Visual direction chosen 2026-07-13 after three rejected prototypes (card grid "too messy", plain column "bleak/basic", route-trace "cartoonish"): **"Dossier"** — Tufte-style quiet reading column: single 62ch serif column (Charter), mono marginalia (timestamps/types) in a left margin column, sparkline-scale clickable timeline, no cards/shadows/ornament, one accent (validated blue #2a78d6/#3987e5) + correction red. Rejected alternatives presented: Swiss editorial grid, dark split-pane console. Prototype artifact: https://claude.ai/code/artifact/bacb0722-c254-4b1c-bff9-85c1899136c9

- Research report published as artifact: https://claude.ai/code/artifact/a755bb8f-a718-4415-accd-cb78c58b45d8 (source in session scratchpad; not yet saved to a project dir — no project dir/name chosen yet).

Key citations: AgentLens (arXiv 2402.08995, closest prior system, eval 68→92% tracing accuracy), ClearFairy (arXiv 2509.14537, 85% LLM-inferred rationale acceptance), Wikum (CSCW 2017), Ragan provenance framework (VAST 2015), "How Coding Agents Fail Their Users" (arXiv 2605.29442).

metadata: 
  node_type: memory

New project (2026-07-13): app for fast human review of a Claude Code session — extract design decisions and let a reviewer trace them. North star: fast human understanding. Deliberately kept **separate** from [[project-session-decision-tree]] (which may later plug in as a "lens").

Decisions made 2026-07-13:
…[truncated]

lines: - Hybrid analysis: deterministic parsing for MVP1 (episodes segmented by user turns, explicit decisions from AskUserQuestion/plan approvals, friction from interruptions/corrections), LLM enrichment in Phase 2 (QOC rationale inference, AgentLens-style interval summaries + embedding change-point segmentation, Wikum recursive summary tree).
- Core schema constant across phases: Decision = {question, options, criteria, resolution, decider, evidence_turns} (QOC — MacLean et al. 1991).
- Display: overview+detail (Cockburn 2009) with martini-glass narrative flow (Segel & Heer 2010); linear chronological timeline (Brehmer 2017); audience is both self-recall and cold readers.

-- Visual direction chosen 2026-07-13 after three rejected prototypes (card grid "too messy", plain column "bleak/basic", route-trace "cartoonish"): **"Dossier"** — Tufte-style quiet reading column: single 62ch serif column (Charter), mono marginalia (timestamps/types) in a left margin column, sparkline-scale clickable timeline, no cards/shadows/ornament, one accent (validated blue #2a78d6/#3987e5) + correction red. Rejected alternatives presented: Swiss editorial grid, dark split-pane console. Prototype artifact: https://claude.ai/code/artifact/bacb0722-c254-4b1c-bff9-85c1899136c9

+- Visual layout settled 2026-07-13 by the user's own spec after four rejected prototypes (card grid "too messy", plain column "bleak/basic", route-trace "cartoonish", Tufte reading column "don't like it at all"): **timeline master + detail card** — top half is an interactive horizontal timeline (labeled marks for highlights, faint ticks for every turn, 15-min grid), clicking a mark swaps a QOC detail card below (options/why/source turns, ←→ nav); tape collapsed at bottom. Treatment: sleek/minimal — grotesk type (Helvetica Neue), hairlines, no ornament, validated blue #2a78d6/#3987e5 + correct…[truncated]

- Research report published as artifact: https://claude.ai/code/artifact/a755bb8f-a718-4415-accd-cb78c58b45d8 (source in session scratchpad; not yet saved to a project dir — no project dir/name chosen yet).

Key citations: AgentLens (arXiv 2402.08995, closest prior system, eval 68→92% tracing accuracy), ClearFairy (arXiv 2509.14537, 85% LLM-inferred rationale acceptance), Wikum (CSCW 2017), Ragan provenance framework (VAST 2015), "How Coding Agents Fail Their Users" (arXiv 2605.29442).

name: project-session-review
description: "Session Review app — helps a human quickly understand decisions in a Claude Code session; research phase done 2026-07-13, no code yet"
metadata:

New project (2026-07-13): app for fast human review of a Claude Code session — extract design decisions and let a reviewer trace them. North star: fast human understanding. Deliberately kept **separate** from [[project-session-decision-tree]] (which may later plug in as a "lens").

Decisions made 2026-07-13:
…[truncated]

lines: - Hybrid analysis: deterministic parsing for MVP1 (episodes segmented by user turns, explicit decisions from AskUserQuestion/plan approvals, friction from interruptions/corrections), LLM enrichment in Phase 2 (QOC rationale inference, AgentLens-style interval summaries + embedding change-point segmentation, Wikum recursive summary tree).
- Core schema constant across phases: Decision = {question, options, criteria, resolution, decider, evidence_turns} (QOC — MacLean et al. 1991).
- Display: overview+detail (Cockburn 2009) with martini-glass narrative flow (Segel & Heer 2010); linear chronological timeline (Brehmer 2017); audience is both self-recall and cold readers.

-- Visual layout settled 2026-07-13 by the user's own spec after four rejected prototypes (card grid "too messy", plain column "bleak/basic", route-trace "cartoonish", Tufte reading column "don't like it at all"): **timeline master + detail card** — top half is an interactive horizontal timeline (labeled marks for highlights, faint ticks for every turn, 15-min grid), clicking a mark swaps a QOC detail card below (options/why/source turns, ←→ nav); tape collapsed at bottom. Treatment: sleek/minimal — grotesk type (Helvetica Neue), hairlines, no ornament, validated blue #2a78d6/#3987e5 + correct…[truncated]
